description: The provided image is a non-scientific placeholder icon displaying a bookmark symbol inside a circle with the text 'Check for updates' beneath it, rendering it unreadable as a materials science figure. No experimental micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectroscopic data, or quantitative curves are visible within the image pixels. The accompanying text extraction summary concerns Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel subjected to cold rolling reductions ranging from 10% to 90%, characterized by optical microscopy, TEM, EBSD, XRD, and uniaxial tensile testing; however, none of these scientific elements appear in the current image. Because the figure lacks discernible sample labels, scale bars, coordinate axes, phase maps, or microstructural features, its specific role in supporting the paper’s conclusions—such as the evolution from Taylor lattices to microbands, nano-twins, shear bands and dislocation cells, the texture transition from copper-type to brass-type, or the increase in yield strength from ~570 MPa to ~2000 MPa—cannot be visually verified from the image alone.

This figure presents room-temperature tensile mechanical data for Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density austenitic steel subjected to various cold rolling reductions (0%, 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, 70%, and 90%). Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves, illustrating a pronounced strength-ductility trade-off: the as-quenched state (0%) shows the lowest strength but the highest uniform elongation, whereas increasing cold rolling reduction progressively raises both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength up to approximately 2 GPa at 90% reduction while drastically diminishing ductility and work-hardening capacity. Subfigure (b) shows the corresponding true stress-strain curves, where hollow circles mark the necking points connected by dashed lines. Citation text reports that the true stress at necking remains comparable at low-to-medium reductions (<50%) but sharply increases upon further rolling, indicating a transition in deformation mechanisms. Collectively, these curves demonstrate the evolution from a highly ductile austenitic matrix to an ultra-high-strength material with limited uniform elongation, supporting the core investigation of microstructural and textural evolution during cold rolling.

Figure 2 displays the initial microstructure of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel in the as-quenched condition before cold rolling. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure map relative to the rolling direction (RD), showing equiaxed austenite grains with black and red lines denoting high-angle grain boundaries (≥15°) and twin boundaries, respectively, over a 200 μm field of view. Subfigure (b) presents the corresponding EBSD phase map in which austenite is colored blue, confirming the fully austenitic matrix with extensive annealing twins. Subfigure (c) is a TEM dark-field image revealing nano-sized κ′-carbides uniformly dispersed in the matrix, accompanied by a selected-area diffraction pattern (inset) with the foil normal parallel to [001]γ (scale bar 50 nm). Collectively, these images establish that the starting material consists of large equiaxed austenite grains with an almost random crystallographic texture, abundant annealing twins, and uniformly distributed nano-precipitates, providing the baseline microstructure for subsequent cold-rolling studies.

Figure 3 presents optical micrographs showing the microstructure evolution of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel subjected to cold rolling reductions of (a) 10%, (b) 30%, (c) 50%, (d) 70%, and (e) 90%. Each micrograph includes a 100 μm scale bar. Insets in (a) and (b) reveal magnified details of slip band arrangements within individual grains. Subfigures (c)–(e) are oriented with the normal direction (ND) vertical and rolling direction (RD) horizontal, showing progressive grain elongation along RD. The caption and citation context describe two deformation stages: Stage I (10%–50%) exhibits a Taylor-lattice-type low energy dislocation structure (LEDS) formed by planar dislocation glide, with gradually refined slip band spacing and inhomogeneous deformation. Stage II (70%–90%) is characterized by abundant shear bands, visibly elongated grains along RD, and progressive shear band proliferation that increasingly erodes the matrix. The figure visually supports the transition from planar slip dominance to shear band development during cold rolling, which contributes to the texture evolution from Copper-type to Brass-type.

This figure presents two TEM bright-field micrographs showing the dislocation substructure evolution in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density austenitic steel during early-stage cold rolling. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure after 10% cold rolling reduction, revealing a Taylor-lattice-type low-energy dislocation structure composed of multiple parallel slip bands. Green arrows indicate regions of dislocation pile-up at the intersections of slip bands, yet the interaction between non-coplanar slip bands remains weak, allowing dislocations to glide across intersections. Subfigure (b) shows the microstructure after 30% cold rolling reduction, where the density of slip bands visibly increases while the overall Taylor-lattice character persists without a qualitative change in deformation mode. The scale bar is 500 nm for both images. The accompanying text reports that the average slip band spacing at 30% reduction is approximately 231 nm, reflecting progressive refinement via planar dislocation glide. Together, these images demonstrate that at low strains (true stress below ~1.2 GPa), microstructural evolution is dominated by the refinement of slip band spacing within the Taylor lattice, consistent with a 'glide plane softening' mechanism rather than strong dislocation blocking.

TEM micrographs and selected-area diffraction showing the deformation substructure of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel after 50% cold rolling reduction. (a) Bright-field image revealing microbands (marked by green dashed lines) with an average width of ~166 nm, indicating significant misorientation across boundaries. (b) Bright-field image showing intermittent linear structures arranged in parallel within some grains. (c) Selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP) from the linear structures in (b) with foil normal near [0 1 1], exhibiting spots indexed as (1-11) of the matrix, (-111) of the twin, and the shared (111)T/M reflection, confirming these structures as nano-deformation twins. (d) Dark-field image using the (-111)T reflection from (c), visualizing extremely fine nano-twins with an average thickness of ~5.1 nm. The diffraction contrast variation within individual twins implies a high dislocation density inside them. Together, these images demonstrate that at 50% reduction, the deformation mechanism evolves from planar slip to microband formation and nano-twinning, supporting the texture transition from Copper-type to Brass-type.

Figure 6 presents TEM micrographs revealing the deformation substructure evolution in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling. Subfigure (a) is a dark-field image showing nano-twins in the sample subjected to 70% cold rolling reduction; the inset selected-area diffraction pattern exhibits a spot pattern with extra reflections consistent with twinning, and the text reports the twin size increased to ~19.2 nm. Subfigure (b) is a bright-field image from the same 70% rolled condition, highlighting shear bands alongside deformation twins; the inset SAED pattern labeled A displays concentric diffraction rings, which the text confirms indicates the shear bands comprise ultrafine grains. Subfigure (c) is a bright-field image after 90% cold rolling reduction, showing dense dislocation tangles and cell structures; the text reports an average dislocation tangle size of ~0.49 μm. Collectively, these images demonstrate that increasing rolling reduction drives a transition from nano-twinning and shear-band formation toward dislocation-dominated substructures, supporting localized grain refinement within shear bands and the eventual activation of cross-slip at higher strains.

This figure presents a schematic illustration of the main crystallographic texture components in Euler space for the investigated Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling, serving as a reference for ODF interpretation. The composite image contains three φ2 constant sections. The left panel (φ2 = 0°) maps the α-fiber (dashed horizontal line) with marked ideal orientations: Cube (C, square), Goss (G, filled diamond), Brass (B, diamond), and Rotated Goss (RtG, hexagon), plotted against the Φ (vertical) and φ1 (horizontal) axes. The central panel (φ2 = 45°) depicts the τ-fiber (horizontal dashed arrow) and γ-fiber (vertical dashed arrow), identifying positions of E (open downward triangle), F (filled downward triangle), Copper (Cu, open upward triangle), Copper Twin (CuT, filled upward triangle), RtG, B, and G components. The right panel (φ2 = 65°) indicates additional orientations labeled S and O. These diagrams provide the benchmark framework for analyzing XRD-measured texture evolution, supporting the reported transition from copper-type to brass-type texture and the progressive strengthening of the α-fiber with increasing cold rolling reduction.

This figure presents selected Orientation Distribution Function (ODF) sections at φ2 = 0°, 45°, and 65° for Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel subjected to cold rolling reductions of 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90%. The contour maps display texture intensity in multiples of random distribution (MRD), ranging from 1 (grey) to 9 (red), revealing the evolution of crystallographic texture components during deformation. At φ2 = 0°, the α-fiber running from Goss (G) to Brass (B) progressively intensifies with increasing reduction. At φ2 = 45°, the copper (Cu) and copper-twin (CuT) components weaken after 50% reduction. At φ2 = 65°, the S component emerges around 50% reduction. At 90% reduction, a weak γ-fiber near the F and E ({111}〈110〉) components is visible. The caption and citation indicate a transition from copper-type to brass-type texture occurs at approximately 50% cold rolling reduction, characterized by the gradual decrease of Cu and S components alongside monotonic strengthening of α-fiber, reflecting the combined effects of planar dislocation glide and deformation twinning in this high-Mn austenitic steel.

This figure presents the X-ray-diffraction-based ODF texture-fiber evolution in Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C austenitic low-density steel cold-rolled to 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 % reduction. Panels (a)–(d) show the orientation density f(g) along the α-fiber (<110>//ND, Φ=45°, φ2=0°), τ-fiber (<110>//TD, φ1=90°, φ2=45°), γ-fiber (<111>//ND, Φ=55°, φ2=45°), and β-fiber (as a function of φ2), respectively. In (a), the Brass ({110}<112>) component strengthens with reduction and scatters toward Goss ({110}<100>), while the peak φ1 shifts from 45° toward ~20° at reductions above 50 %. In (b), the Cu ({112}<111>) intensity peaks at 50 % then declines, accompanied by a Φ shift to higher angles and a rapid increase in Goss; a weak {552}<115> CuT component emerges at 70 %. The γ-fiber in (c) remains weak but slightly intensifies at 90 % near the {111}<110> E position. The β-fiber in (d) shows Cu and S components rising then falling while Goss increases monotonously, reflecting a continuous shift from Brass toward Goss. Panels (e) and (f) plot the Φ and φ1 coordinates of the maximum intensity along the β-fiber versus φ2, corroborating the systematic displacement toward Goss with increasing cold rolling. These data support a transition from copper-type to brass-type texture driven by planar slip, deformation twinning, and shear-band activity.
